title: Eva at the Time
wikitext:
[]: Eva at the Time             Quote Post by ehh123 » Fri Aug 22, 2014 4:19 pm When Evangelion first aired, what did people think about Studio Gainax? Did people respect it for Nadia: The Secret of Blue Water ? Was the fact they hired voice actors as big as Kotono Mitsuishi (Usagi/Sailor Moon) and Megumi Hayashibara (Female Ranma and Lina Inverse) all that impressive? I like looking at this kind of historical context when it comes to groundbreaking anime. ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Ultrahuntr » Tue Oct 07, 2014 4:10 pm I don't have anything to add to this thread, but I'm really disappointed it didn't take off. I would have really liked to know the details of this.

[]:             Quote Post by Grand Duke of Yashima » Tue Oct 07, 2014 5:22 pm I always got the impression that back in the day GAINAX was considered the "Otaku's studio," given as so much of the works put out pre-EVA by GAINAX were otaku-friendly and loaded with not only subtle and not-so-subtle references to other anime and sci-fi works, but also because GAINAX put in the extra mile to do things such as "Jiggle Physics" that certainly otakus (and non-otakus alike) could appreciate. I remember going to AnimeCon '91 in San Jose and seeing many of the GAINAX anime titles there for the first time, such as Nadia , Gunbuster , and Royal Space Force. The impression you got from nearly all of the things they were making was that GAINAX had a sort of "no compromises" approach to their projects. Backgrounds were lush with detail, character and mecha designs were highly impressive, and they got the best voice talent they could get at the time. Of course all of that was bloody expensive and the GAINAX merchandising machine was already alive and kicking long before EVA. They definitely stood out at the time among the other anime studios, and while their appeal was to a more narrow demographic of the anime fandom in Japan, it was tailor-made to the hard-core otakus and as a result they had one of the more enthusiastic followings. They weren't family-oriented like Ghibli but kept Ghibli's strong attention to detail, something that you didn't really see from contemporaries such as Sunrise, Studio Perriot, and AIC. I think the Notenki Memoirs are pretty key in understanding the GAINAX story and the whole background behind making EVA. Also, if you can get your hands on it, definitely watch Otaku No Video which is an amazing GAINAX-sort of autobiographical work done right before they went BK in the early 90s.

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Tue Oct 07, 2014 6:36 pm I was in kindergarten when Eva was first airing. The main thing on my mind was Nicktoons.

[]:             Quote Post by Dataprime » Tue Oct 07, 2014 7:34 pm Guy Nacks wrote: I was in kindergarten when Eva was first airing. The main thing on my mind was Nicktoons.
